data.title: Europe before the bell: STOXX heads for gains
data.description:
EUROPE BEFORE THE BELL: STOXX HEADS FOR GAINS

European stocks are set to bounce back at the open on Thursday, tracking Wednesday's late gains in the U.S., after U.S. President Donald Trump backed down from threats to impose tariffs as leverage to seize Greenland, ruling out the use of force and hinting a deal was in sight.

Worries about Greenland had been weighing on share markets around the world this week, and the main STOXX 600 index has fallen 1.9%.

But things are set to change at the open, and Euro STOXX 50 futures are adding 0.8%, while futures on the DAX are up 0.9% and FTSE futures are 0.6% higher.

In company news, Volkswagen has reported better-than-expected net cash flow in 2025, while French market research firm Ipsos said it plans to invest 1.2 billion euros ($1.4 billion) over five years in artificial intelligence and acquisitions.

Spain's Bankinter said fourth-quarter net profit rose 25% from the same period in 2024, helped by a rise in fees.

British retailers are under the spotlight. Following a profit warning earlier this month, Associated British Foods confirmed underlying sales at its Primark clothing business fell 2.7 % in the Christmas quarter. Meanwhile B&M has cut its profit forecast.

data.title: Borrowing data offers early new year cheer for UK's Reeves
data.description:
By Andy Bruce

Britain's government borrowed less than expected in December, helped by robust growth in tax receipts, according to data on Thursday that may give finance minister Rachel Reeves reasons for optimism in 2026.

Britain's government borrowed 11.578 billion pounds ($15.55 billion) in December, the Office for National Statistics said.

Economists polled by Reuters had mostly expected 13.0 billion pounds of public sector net borrowing last month.

Reeves has faced a challenging year-and-a-half in charge of Britain's economy. She was forced to increase taxes again in her November budget to build a larger buffer against her fiscal rules, as she sought to retain the confidence of investors.

"The December figures offer cautious reassurance. Borrowing remains elevated in absolute terms, but the trend is moving in the right direction," said Joe Nellis, economic adviser at MHA, an accountancy and advisory firm.

Borrowing over the first nine months of the 2025/26 financial year stood at 140.4 billion pounds, compared with 140.8 billion pounds at the same point last year, the ONS said.

In November, the Office for Budget Responsibility projected borrowing for the entire 2025/26 financial year of 138.3 billion pounds, which takes into account an expected January seasonal surplus.

Both tax receipts and day-to-day government spending are running 7.6% higher at this point of the financial year compared with 2024/25.

The picture differs markedly from January 2025, when gilt yields soared during a global government bond sell-off that forced Reeves to repeatedly reassure investors of her commitment to fiscal discipline.

Gilt auctions held during January 2026 have shown some of the strongest demand for British debt on record.

($1 = 0.7452 pounds)

data.title: Getlink Q4 Revenue Improves; Expects Annual EBITDA To Exceed Previous Guidance
data.description:
PARIS (dpa-AFX) - Getlink SE (GRPTY, GET.PA), a European provider of infrastructure management and transportation services, on Thursday recorded an increase in revenue for the fourth quarter.

For the fourth quarter, the company registered revenue of EUR 384 million, higher than the restated EUR 325 million in the same period last year. Revenue from Eurotunnel stood at EUR 270 million, up from the restated EUR 261 million in the previous year.

For fiscal 2025, Getlink now anticipates its EBITDA to exceed its earlier outlook of EUR 780 million to EUR 830 million. This revised outlook reflects the deal reached with insurance companies regarding compensation for Eleclink's operating losses in 2024.

Getlink will release its annual earnings report on February 26.

(07:34 GMT) Daimler Truck's Japan Unit, Foxconn to Set Up Electric Bus Maker

By Kosaku Narioka

Daimler Truck Holding's Japan unit and Foxconn Technology Group plan to set up a bus maker in the second half of this year, to develop and sell electric buses in Japan and internationally.

The Daimler Truck unit, Mitsubishi Fuso Truck & Bus, and Foxconn said Thursday that the new company would combine Fuso's expertise in design, development and manufacturing of buses with Foxconn's zero-emission vehicle technology and global network.

Fuso Bus head Katsuto Kora will be the chief executive of the new company, which will be based in Kawasaki, Japan, the companies said.

(07:07 GMT) *B&M European Value Retail: Lowering Guidance on Deeper Investment in Future Growth
(07:34 GMT) B&M European Value Retail Cuts Guidance

By Nina Kienle

B&M European Value Retail said it cut its fiscal 2026 adjusted earnings guidance due to growing investments, and despite posting higher third-quarter revenue.

The U.K discount retailer on Thursday said it now expects fiscal 2026 group adjusted earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization to be between 440 million and 475 million pounds ($590.9 million - $637.9 million). It had previously targeted a range of between 470 million and 520 million pounds.

The guidance cut is driven by continued investments in pricing and clearance, as well as improvements in stock quality and weakness in Heron Foods, it said.

B&M also said its group revenue rose 2.9% on-year to 1.74 billion pounds.

The company's third quarter covers the 13-week period to Dec. 27, which encompasses the Christmas key business period.

B&M's U.K. division reported revenue rise of 1.9% to 1.41 billion pounds while B&M France reported an 8.5% on-year rise in revenue to 186 million pounds. Heron Foods reported a 1.4% rise in revenue to 138 million pounds.

Write to Nina Kienle at nina.kienle@wsj.com
